questions.question: What is the main difference between relative depth and metric depth?
options: Relative depth is always lower resolution
There is no difference
Metric depth uses grayscale output; relative uses colour
Relative depth gives only ordered distances without real-world units; metric depth gives distances in metres and requires the model to have learnt absolute scale from training data
questions.explanation: Relative depth preserves ordering and ratios but has no anchor to real-world units — a MiDaS or Depth Anything prediction needs alignment before you can compare it to a ground-truth measurement in metres. Metric depth models (ZoeDepth, UniDepth, Metric3D) output calibrated distances at the cost of sensitivity to camera intrinsics and training data coverage.

questions.question: A texture-gradient cue tells a monocular depth model that a surface is receding. Which description best defines texture gradient?
options: Parallel lines in 3D converge toward a vanishing point in the image
A uniform surface appears to have progressively smaller and denser texture elements as it recedes from the camera
Distant objects appear hazier and slightly bluer due to atmospheric scattering
Nearer objects overlap and occlude farther ones
questions.explanation: Texture gradient is the perceptual cue where a surface's repeated elements — pebbles, grass blades, brickwork — project to ever-smaller and denser patterns at greater distances. A ViT encoder trained on billions of images internalises this cue alongside perspective convergence, occlusion ordering, size constancy, and atmospheric perspective, allowing monocular depth to work without any 3D sensor.

questions.question: AbsRel is defined as mean(|d_pred − d_gt| / d_gt). Why divide by d_gt rather than computing a raw absolute error mean(|d_pred − d_gt|)?
options: Dividing by d_gt makes the error scale-invariant — a 10 cm error at 1 m (10%) is treated the same as a 1 m error at 10 m (10%), preventing distant pixels from dominating the metric
d_pred can be zero, causing division by zero in the alternative
Dividing by d_gt is a legacy convention with no mathematical motivation
It prevents the metric from penalising overestimates more than underestimates
questions.explanation: Raw absolute error biases the metric toward large distances where absolute values are bigger. Dividing by d_gt normalises to a percentage at each pixel, so near and far scene elements contribute equally. AbsRel near 0.05–0.1 is the standard production target; values above 0.2 indicate poor generalisation. Scale-and-shift alignment is applied first for relative-depth models before computing AbsRel.

questions.question: The lesson's `align_scale_shift(pred, target)` function stacks `[pred, ones]` into matrix A and calls `torch.linalg.lstsq(A, target)`. Which linear model is it fitting?
options: pred / target = a, with b = 0 (scale-only fit)
a + b * pred = target (intercept-first convention)
a * pred + b = target (scale a and shift b that minimise squared error)
a * (pred − b)² = target (quadratic alignment)
questions.explanation: `align_scale_shift` solves a * pred + b = target via least squares: the two-column design matrix A = [pred, 1] lets lstsq recover coefficients [a, b]^T. This corrects the global scale and offset ambiguity that relative-depth models cannot resolve — a MiDaS prediction may be 5× the true scale and shifted by 0.3 m. Applying the alignment before AbsRel gives a fair evaluation of the model's ordering quality.

questions.question: To lift a pixel (u, v) with predicted depth d to 3D, you use X = (u - cx) * d / fx, Y = (v - cy) * d / fy, Z = d. What are fx, fy, cx, cy?
options: Pinhole camera intrinsics — focal lengths and principal point in pixels, from EXIF metadata or camera calibration
Training loss weights
Normalisation constants
Weights learned by the depth decoder
questions.explanation: The pinhole-camera formula needs the camera intrinsics. fx, fy are the focal lengths in pixel units; cx, cy are the principal point (usually near image centre). Without intrinsics you can only assume a generic FOV (~60 degrees) which is enough for visualisation but not for measurement. Many 2026 pipelines estimate intrinsics automatically from image content (Perspective Fields, UniDepth).

questions.question: When evaluating a relative-depth model (MiDaS, Depth Anything), why do you apply scale-and-shift alignment before computing AbsRel?
options: To inflate the metric score

Relative-depth predictions have arbitrary scale and offset; aligning them to ground truth via least-squares fit of a * pred + b = target gives a fair measurement of the ordering quality that relative models are actually trained to produce
To enable GPU inference
PyTorch cannot compute AbsRel without alignment
questions.explanation: MiDaS-style models produce outputs where only the ordering and ratios are meaningful. Directly computing AbsRel without alignment measures the scale mismatch, not the model's actual quality. Least-squares alignment fits a linear transform that minimises squared error; the residual after alignment is what you report. Every MiDaS / Depth Anything paper uses this protocol.
